title: The Evening and the Morning Star

blocks.text: The Evening and the Morning Star

The first concerted effort by the Church to print the revelations of Joseph Smith occurred in Independence, Jackson County, Missouri. John Whitmer and Oliver Cowdery carried the manuscript Book of Commandments and Revelations to Missouri as directed by the Lord and by the Hiram, Ohio, conference of November 1831. There they intended for William W. Phelps to print the revelations in book form on the press he had established in Independence.

In early 1832, after arriving in Independence, Oliver wrote to Joseph Smith. “We expect soon to be ready to print,” he explained, expressing hope that Martin Harris would be able to supply them with paper.

Paper was not easy to find because Independence was on the American frontier. They set up the press “within 12 miles of the west line of the state of Missouri,” which at the time was “the western limits of the United States.” Presses in America sometimes borrowed paper from one another, but even that was difficult since their new print shop was located “about 120 miles west of any press in the state.”

When Oliver wrote his letter to Joseph, he still expected that they would publish ten thousand copies of the revelations in book form. But that would not be the only function of the new press. In February 1832, William Phelps issued a prospectus for a newspaper, The Evening and the Morning Star. The prospectus explained that the paper would “be devoted to the revelations of God as made known to his servants by the Holy Ghost, at sundry times since the creation of man, but more especially in these last days.” Because the editors had access to the Book of Commandments and Revelations, they could feature revelations from it in the monthly newspaper as they worked on the larger project of printing them in book form.

They mailed a copy of the prospectus to Joseph Smith, who was preparing to visit them in Missouri. Before he left, a mob attacked him in the night, dragging him from his home, choking him into unconsciousness, and then coating him in hot tar and feathers. The mob brutalized his associate Sidney Rigdon too. On their trip to Missouri, Joseph, Sidney, and their companions went to Wheeling, Virginia (now West Virginia), where they “purchased a lot of Paper for the Press in Zion.”

After they reached Missouri, they held a meeting of the Literary Firm, a group of Church leaders directed by revelation a few months earlier “to be stewards over the revelations and commandments.” The group included Joseph Smith, Sidney Rigdon, and the three members in Zion assigned to print the revelations: William W. Phelps, Oliver Cowdery, and John Whitmer.

The Literary Firm reached several conclusions during its meetings. One was “that all Revelations be limited to the parties concerned until printed.” The group also ordered that the three members residing in Missouri—William, Oliver, and John—be responsible to “make all necessary verbal corrections” to the revelations as they prepared them for publication.

Joseph left Independence to return to Ohio in May. In June, the first number of The Evening and the Morning Star issued from the press in Zion. The first article in the newspaper appeared under the heading “Revelations” and was titled “The Articles and Covenants of the Church of Christ.” It began with the revelation later known as Doctrine and Covenants 20, followed by the one that became section 22.

The Articles and Covenants were, in effect, the Church’s spiritual constitution and would be amended by revelation from time to time as the Church grew and a more complex organization was required to operate it.

The first issue of the Star also included an article titled “A Prophecy Given to the Church of Christ, March 7, 1831.” This extract came from what was later known as Doctrine and Covenants 45, verses 1 to 67 and 71.

When William Phelps, Oliver Cowdery, and John Whitmer were directed by the Literary Firm to “make all necessary verbal corrections” to the revelations, Joseph Smith intended that they merely correct misspellings and grammar and make cosmetic changes to prepare them for publication. In a letter to William Phelps sent not long after Joseph returned to Ohio, the Prophet reinforced his intent. “I will exhort you,” he wrote, “to be careful not to alter the sense of any of them for he that adds or diminishes to the prophecies must come under the condemnation written therein.”

Most of the revelations published in The Evening and the Morning Star were typeset from the Book of Commandments and Revelations manuscript carried to Missouri by Oliver and John, though some were not. Some were set from unknown sources, and others match the type that had already been set for the book of Joseph Smith’s revelations being printed at the same time in the shop. That printed book would be called A Book of Commandments.

Some revelations published in the Star were not in the manuscript Book of Commandments and Revelations when Oliver and John left Ohio because they had not yet been received. They were later copied into the manuscript, some perhaps when Joseph Smith visited Missouri in the spring of 1832.

One of these was the vision received by Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon on February 16, 1832, which has been known to later generations as Doctrine and Covenants 76. When this revelation was copied into the Kirtland Revelation Book in Ohio by Frederick G. Williams, it bore the title by which it was known at the time, “The Vision.” But when John Whitmer copied it into the Book of Commandments and Revelations in Missouri, he omitted the title, perhaps because it was missing from the manuscript he copied.

Following the title in the Kirtland Revelation Book, the text began, “A vision of Joseph & Sidney.” John Whitmer wrote “A Vision of Joseph and Sidney” as the title of the revelation when he copied it. When printing the revelation in The Evening and the Morning Star, the typesetter shortened that title to “A Vision,” a more apt title than “The Vision” because Joseph had received many other visions before that one.

Altogether, during its fourteen months of publication in Missouri, The Evening and the Morning Star printed twenty-six revelations, nineteen in full and seven in part. In addition, the paper ran articles that quoted from Joseph Smith’s revelations, some of which were not otherwise printed in the Star.

For many members of the Church, the publication of Joseph Smith’s revelations in The Evening and the Morning Star represented their first opportunity to see the revelations in print. Moreover, most of the revelations published in the Star contained matter relevant to all members of the Church, not just to specific individuals.

The publication of the revelations in the Star was an important step forward in making them available to members of the Church and the world at large. But it was merely a precursor to the greater project of making the revelations available in book form.
